name: Gary Burton
abstract: Gary Burton (born January 23, 1943) is an American retired jazz vibraphonist, composer, and educator. Burton developed a pianistic style of four-mallet technique as an alternative to the prevailing two-mallet technique.

quotes.text: “Raish” [PHONETIC] is in there somewhere. Absolutely. When I started out, I was always the youngest guy in the band. I was 17 years old when I made my first records. I was 19 when I joined my first name band, George Shearing; 20 when I joined Stan Getz. So at some point, instead of everybody else being 40 and me being 20, it shifted the other way. I became the older guy, and the band kept staying younger. I thought about this a lot, and I realized I was influenced by Stan, who also had a history of finding and discovering young players throughout his career. It began long before me and Chick came along. He was inspired by the presence of young, enthusiastic, still not yet jaded younger musicians. So I discovered that you sort of fall into one of two categories. You’re either a musician, as a mature one, who prefers playing with other settled, polished, mature players, and that’s where you feel most comfortable, or you like the unsettled situation of younger players full of surprises and constant shifting and changing and experimenting that goes on. That’s me. I provided that role for Stan when I was young, and I thrived on it all through my adult career, let’s say, by finding young players that just made it great to go to work. Because they’re excited about going to work every night at that age. Older players often aren’t. “Oh, it’s a gig now; it’s a good gig, nothing else I’d rather do, but this is my, you know, 20th time to play Carnegie Hall— it’s no big deal.” A new musician, a young musician says, “Wow, this is fantastic. Can’t wait to get started,” and so on. That rubs off on you if you’re the older musician in the band. I’ve always been drawn to that.
quotes.caption: Gary Burton reflects on playing Carnegie Hall repeatedly
quotes.doc_title: Gary Burton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

quotes.text: I left Stan and wanted to start a new band. My idea was to somehow combine rock music with jazz. I got that idea in sort of a calculated business way, which is... Normally you don’t get great ideas that way. But I was trying to be very practical, and I said: Ok, all my audiences up to now have been with Stan Getz and George Shearing, and these are all people in their forties and fifties, and I’m 23-24. So by the time I’m 40 and 50, they’re going to be dead. So I need to somehow connect with people my own age. So what are they listening to? And I asked myself, “What am I listening to?” Well, I had become a huge Beatles fan, and Bob Dylan and so on, around that time. In fact, I even took the entire Stan Getz family and the entire Stan Getz band to see the Beatles at Shea Stadium, on their final New York-American concert. I talked everybody into going. We got a box seat for the whole gang of us, and the Getz family and the kids and everything, and it was an amazing experience. We actually even were invited to go meet them. Because the promoter was the same promoter that Stan often played dates at Carnegie Hall for and so on—Sid Bernstein. But we said, “oh, it will be really crowded and everything; we’ll have a chance to meet them some other time.” Missed my chance!
quotes.caption: Recalling attending the Beatles at Shea Stadium
quotes.doc_title: Gary Burton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Chick Corea
about_quotes.evidence: I recorded the Tadd Dameron tune, “Hot House,” with Gary Burton recently... I’ve also been on tour with Gary Burton and a string quartet... Manfred Eicher became a help to the duet because he was the first one who suggested that we do a recording, which resulted in our first record, Crystal Silence.
about_quotes.doc_title: Chick Corea — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Bley Carla
about_quotes.evidence: Gary Burton wanted to do an album... I wrote Gary Burton’s quartet into it... we practiced it, we rehearsed it out in California... and recorded it in New York.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bley Carla — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Goodwin
about_quotes.evidence: I was actually that year that Gary called me to play... when I wasn’t on the road with Gary.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bill Goodwin interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, May 28, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Roy Haynes
about_quotes.evidence: Then I rejoin Stan Getz. ... That’s when Gary Burton’s there. ... We take a picture, a group picture. ... So there’s some importance in it. Now I’m playing.
about_quotes.doc_title: Roy Haynes — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Powell
about_quotes.evidence: And the last is Lionel Hampton, Gary Burton and myself... I am in the company of all my people that I’ve idolized for years: ... Gary Burton
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Powell interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 16, 1999 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Wendy Oxenhorn
about_quotes.evidence: I haven’t had a chance to really absorb it, until your director just said what he said about sharing a category with... Say it again.
about_quotes.doc_title: Wendy Oxenhorn — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
